---
title: "De-identification & Synthetic Data"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} · **Methods & Study Design** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Methods & Study Design track](methods-study-design.qmd){.track-back}

*Two ways to work safely with sensitive data: remove identifiers, or generate realistic fakes.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Apply de-identification standards (e.g., HIPAA Safe Harbor)
- Generate synthetic data for sharing and teaching
- Recognize re-identification and fidelity limits

## Overview

De-identification removes identifiers so data can be used more freely; synthetic data generates realistic but fake records. Both let you teach, demo, and prototype without exposing real people — your course already uses synthetic crash data this way.

Neither is magic. De-identified data can sometimes be re-identified by combining fields, and synthetic data can leak patterns or miss real structure. Check both before relying on them.

## Practice activities

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 1 · Moderate — De-identify a sample

1. Take a small sample with quasi-identifiers (age, ZIP, date).
2. Apply **Safe Harbor-style** removal/generalization with AI's help.
3. Ask: could remaining fields still re-identify someone in combination?
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 2 · Moderate — Generate synthetic data

1. Describe a dataset's structure and ask AI to generate a **synthetic** version.
2. Compare distributions to the real structure; note what's off.
:::

## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "De-identified data can still be risky because…", "options": ["Combined fields can re-identify people", "It's always safe", "It's encrypted", "It's synthetic"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Quasi-identifiers can re-identify in combination."}, {"q": "Synthetic data is useful for…", "options": ["Teaching, demos, and prototyping", "Replacing real analysis", "Hiding results", "Avoiding all rules"], "answer": 0, "explain": "It's safe for teaching and prototyping, with limits."}, {"q": "Before relying on either, you should…", "options": ["Check re-identification risk and fidelity", "Assume it's fine", "Publish immediately", "Skip review"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Both have limits worth checking."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **LLMs Can Unmask Pseudonymous Users at Scale (Ars Technica)** — re-identification risk made concrete.
- **AI and Humans: Making the Relationship Work (Schneier on Security)** — security mindset for data handling.

## Useful resources

- [HHS De-identification Guidance](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/special-topics/de-identification/index.html) — HIPAA Safe Harbor and Expert Determination.
- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Generate/transform on **non-sensitive** samples in unapproved tools; real PHI needs an approved tool and method. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
